title: Voltammetric Characterization of Carbon Paste Electrodes with a Nonconducting Binder. Part I: Evidence of the Influence of Electroactive Species Dissolution into the Paste on the Voltammetric Response
abstract:
Voltammetric Characterization of Carbon Paste Electrodes with a
Nonconducting Binder. Part I: Evidence of the Inﬂuence of
Electroactive Species Dissolution into the Paste on the Voltammetric
Response
Ruth Martı´nez, Maria Teresa Ramı´rez,* and Ignacio Gonza´lez
Universidad Auto´noma Metropolitana-Iztapalapa, Departamento de Quı´mica, Apartado Postal 55-534, 09340, Me´xico, D.F., Me´xico
Received: November 10, 1997
Final version: February 12, 1998
Abstract
The voltammetric characterization of ferrocene conﬁned within a carbon paste electrode (CPE) with nonconducting binder (Nujol or silicon oil)
was performed. The voltammetric behavior depends directly on the way in which the electroactive species were introduced into the paste: as a solid
or previously dissolved into the binder. These two forms were found to contribute in the electrochemical process in a single voltammetric peak.
Variations in the potential sweep rate , type of binder (meaning changes in ferrocene solubility), the amount of binder in the paste, and also the
amount of the electroactive species affect the shape of the voltammetric curves obtained. This enables us to distinguish between the contributions of
the solid and dissolved forms of ferrocene in the voltammetric response. From this study, it is established that the ferrocene oxidation in CPE was
limited by diffusion, taking place in a layer beyond the electrode–electrolyte interface, resulting from the dissolution of ferrocene within the binder.
The existing controversy reported for voltammetric studies of ferrocene in CPE could be explained by the presence of two different ferrocene
species involved in the oxidation process.
Keywords: Carbon paste electrode, Ferrocene, Pasting liquid
1. Introduction
The carbon paste electrode (CPE) was introduced by Adams [1, 2]
and was originally made of graphite powder and a nonconducting,
liquid binder. Addition of an electroactive substance to the carbon
paste was ﬁrst performed by Kuwana [3] and thereafter reported by
various authors [4–6] who even used electrolytic binder.
Electroactive CPE’s with conductive and nonconductive binders
are widely used in solid state voltammetry. The former of such
electrodes were used in mineral analysis [7, 8] while the latter
electrodes have been employed in a variety of applications such as
analysis of numerous organic compounds [9], determination of
inorganic compounds [10–12] as well as being used as electrodes
with electrocatalytic properties [13–16]. An extensive review of
CPE’s was recently published by Kalcher et al. [17].
Despite the extensive use of CPE’s, the study of the inﬂuence of
their components and construction on the electrochemical response
of the added substance has been largely ignored. In fact, the
importance of the type and amount of binder is rarely mentioned in
the literature [18, 19]. Presumably, the only purpose of this liquid is
to aid in the practical manipulation of the electrode, without
affecting the system being tested, so that the optimal relationship
between the binder and the graphite powder is only determined by
the desired paste consistency. Any electroinactive liquid of low
volatility and solubility in water and that can be obtained free of
electroactive impurities can be used [8, 9, 20].
Our study shows that the role of these parameters is not
necessarily
trivial
and
demonstrates
cases
where
solubility
phenomena take place in the interior of the paste, due to the
dissolution of the electroactive species in the binder. Although
Kuwana [4] has reported the electrochemical behavior of species
initially dissolved in the nonconducting binder of a CPE, few
studies have examined the inﬂuence of the solubility of the
incorporated solid on the electrochemical response. Using CPE with
a conducting binder, Bauer et al. [5, 6] proposed that the solid
disappears by a progressive dissolution that precedes the electro-
chemical reaction and that only the dissolved species undergoes
transformation. Throughout this study of electroactive CPE’s with
nonconductive binder, we show that both the dissolved species and
the solid are involved in the electrochemical process, and that the
relative contribution of each one is a function of experimental
variables such as the composition of the paste and the method
chosen for preparation.
Ferrocene was chosen as a test system because of its extensive
use and importance in electrochemical reference systems [21–24],
for its role as a redox mediator in enzymatic studies [25] and for its
appropriate solubility properties in nonconducting binders. For our
study we selected the most commonly used of the various
nonconducting binders for CPE’s, Nujol and silicon oils.
Electrochemical studies of ferrocene
tend
to show little
consistency. For voltammograms reported [3–6, 26, 27] the
oxidation potential peak and the shape of the peak often vary
from one study to the next one. In fact researchers have faced
considerable difﬁculty in reproducing reported results. Addition-
ally, the electronic transfer mechanism in these pastes is highly
debated. Some authors have reported that the oxidation process is
governed by diffusion [3, 4] while others reject this notion [28]. It is
clear that more studies need to be done to assess a detailed
description of the mechanism taking place in the electrochemical
process.
2. Experimental
2.1. Electrode Preparation
Two methods were used to prepare the carbon paste:
a) Graphite was mixed with solid ferrocene stirring it with a glass
rod; the binder was added afterwards. The mixture was stirred to
uniform consistency. Different paste compositions were pre-
pared by varying the amount of binder from 0.35 to 0.65 mL and
keeping the amount of ferrocene constant at 0.5 % (w./w.). For
example, a typical mixture would contain 0.995 g of graphite
336
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 5
q WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
1040-0397/98/0504-0336 $ 17.50þ.50/0
 and 0.005 g of ferrocene with varying amounts of binder. For
other pastes, the amount of binder was kept constant and the
ferrocene content was varied from 0.5 % to 3.0 %. This method
of preparation is referred to as fs.
b) For the second method, ferrocene was dissolved into the binder
before the graphite powder was mixed in. Composition was
varied by increasing the binder content from 0.35 to 0.65 mL
keeping the amount of ferrocene constant at 0.5 %. This method
is referred to as fd.
In all cases the paste was packed into a PVC tube with a
geometric surface area of 0.031 cm2 and compacted using a piston.
The electric connection was made by copper wire. The use of
copper wire was decided after preliminary tests showed no
difference in the electrochemical response between a platinum
and a copper wire. The electrodes were used on experimentation
immediately after preparation was completed, showing a repro-
ducible electrochemical behavior. The reproducibility of the
electrochemical response must be a result of both, the homogeneity
of the paste as well as its stability throughout the time of
experimentation. The fact that renovation of the electrode surface
after 8 h of continuous use shows no changes on the electrode
response, conﬁrms its stability.
2.2. Reagents and Equipment
99 % pure graphite powder, 1–2 mm particle size, was purchased
from Aldrich. Ferrocene (Aldrich) was used at 98 % purity as
supplied with no further puriﬁcation. Liquid binders of high purity
were used: silicone oil was purchased from Sigma, with 0.96 g/mL
density, 400 cp viscosity, (refraction index) nD= 1.4058, and Nujol
from Aldrich with 0.842 g/mL density, 62.5 cp viscosity, nD ¼
1.4708. Viscosity values and refraction indexes were determined
experimentally in this work. Cyclic voltammetry studies were
carried out using a Tacussel Bi-Pad Bipotentiostat and a Tacussel
GSTP3 triangular signal generator. Signals were registered on
an X-Y-T Graphtec plotter type wx1000. A PAR Potentiostat-
Galvanostat model 273 and a PAR signal-generator model 175 were
also used coupled to a Hewlett Packard plotter model 7090A.
Studies were carried out at room temperature in a typical, three
electrode cell under inert atmosphere. The CPE was the working
electrode while a mercury sulfate electrode was used as a reference,
and a graphite bar as an auxiliary electrode. A 2 M H2SO4 solution,
prepared with N2 deoxygenated and deionized water, was used as
the support electrolyte. The voltammograms of the undisturbed
solution were recorded immediately after introducing the working
electrode into the electrolyte solution. The electrode surface was
renewed after each sweep by pushing approximately 3 mm of the
paste out of the tube and polishing the new surface on 600 mm
Buhler paper. An average of four repetitions were done for each
potential sweep rate, to assess reproducibility of the response.
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Preparative Studies
A series of preparative studies were carried out to evaluate
ferrocene solubility in Nujol and silicone oil, conﬁrming that a
signiﬁcant quantity of ferrocene was dissolved in the binder.
Species characterization in both Nujol and silicon oil solution was
carried out using UV-vis and IR spectrophotometry. Figure 1
shows the UV-vis spectra. Both spectra gave the same proﬁle
with maximum absorption at 435 nm, establishing that the nature of
the species in solution did not change when using different
binders.
Due to the lack of reference data, ferrocene solubility was
estimated experimentally for the saturated solution using a
calibration plot at 435 nm. No dissolution problems were observed
in the construction of this plot. The saturation concentration of
ferrocene, determined by this method, resulted in 0.15 M and
0.075 M in Nujol and silicone oil, respectively. Because of its
higher hydrocarbon content, Nujol proved to be a better solvent for
ferrocene than silicone oil.
The pastes prepared for this work had a ferrocene concentration
that was smaller than its solubility value. In some pastes
manufactured by fs with high ferrocene content, the ferrocene
concentration in binder was above the solubility value (pastes from
2 % of ferrocene with nujol and from 1 % of ferrocene with silicone
oil).
Due to the fact that the solubility values estimated in the present
work are relatively high, we think that an important dissolution of
solid ferrocene in the nonconductive binder of the CPE can take
place and this can lead to differences in the electrochemical
response when the electrode is manufactured by different methods.
In the study of solid ferrocene in a CPE with nonconductive binder,
the electrochemical response will depend on the solid ferrocene
species, Fc(s), as well as the ferrocene dissolved in the binder
during the manufacturing process, Fc(d). The present work aims to
evaluate the contribution of each ferrocene species. The dissolved
species could promote a diffusional process in the paste during the
oxidation process. For this purpose, a study of the CPE’s
manufactured with a previous dissolution of ferrocene in the
binder (fd) is presented. This is not commonly found in the
literature. And then, a study of CPE’s manufactured as usually
reported [mixing solid ferrocene and graphite together and adding
the binder afterwards (fs)] is also presented.
3.2. Inﬂuence of the Dissolved Ferrocene Species on the
Oxidation Process
Figure 2 shows a typical voltammogram obtained in a CPE with
0.5 % ferrocene. A well-deﬁned peak appears in the anodic region at
¹0.185 V(SME), attributed to the oxidation process of ferrocene to
ferrocinium, while a reduction peak is observed at ¹0.265 V(SME),
337
Carbon Paste Electrodes with Nonconducting Binders
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 5
Fig. 1. Typical absorption spectra in the UV-vis region observed for
dissolved ferrocene in a) Nujol and b) silicon oil.

Figure 2 shows a typical voltammogram obtained in a CPE with
0.5 % ferrocene. A well-deﬁned peak appears in the anodic region at
¹0.185 V(SME), attributed to the oxidation process of ferrocene to
ferrocinium, while a reduction peak is observed at ¹0.265 V(SME),
Carbon Paste Electrodes with Nonconducting Binders
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 5
Fig. 1. Typical absorption spectra in the UV-vis region observed for
dissolved ferrocene in a) Nujol and b) silicon oil.
corresponding to the reverse transformation. The peak potentials
(Ep) in which each process was registered, showed no signiﬁcant
alterations when there were changes in the method of manufacture
or in the amount of binder used in the paste.
The effect of the amount of binder in the paste on the current of
the oxidation peak was studied for both methods used to
manufacture the electrodes. Figure 3 shows the behavior of the
anodic peak current (ipa) versus the square root of the sweep rate
(v1/2) in pastes containing 0.35 to 0.65 mL of Nujol and 0.5 %
ferrocene prepared with: a) ferrocene previously dissolved in the
binder and then mixed with graphite (fd) and, b) solid ferrocene
mixed with graphite followed by addition of the binder (fs). In the
ﬁrst case (Fig. 3a), the current decreases when the amount of binder
is higher. This can be accounted for by a simple dilution effect,
considering the amount of ferrocene is the same in these pastes. For
the second case, (Fig. 3b) the increase in the current when the
amount of binder is higher can be thought of as to be the result of
kinetics of ferrocene dissolution during the electrode manufacturing
process. However, as the previous studies to determine the
saturation concentration showed, ferrocene dissolved easily and
this leads to conclude that kinetic effects are not responsible for this
As the electrochemical response was reproducible throughout our
work, it seems to be that an equilibrium state has been reached at the
end of the manufacturing process of the electrodes. As a possible
hypothesis, it is proposed that a different equilibrium state could
have been reached for each manufacturing process: fs and fd. This
could be the result of a combination of factors such as ferrocene
dissolution, graphite wetting by the binder, etc. that could change
the solid-dissolved ferrocene proportion in the electrode. The linear
function with zero intercept observed in all cases, shows that the
oxidation process is governed by diffusion of one species in
solution; this species could be the dissolved ferrocene in the binder.
In the manufacturing process fs, ferrocene dissolution process
could be affected by the amount of binder needed to wet the
graphite particles. The ferrocene dissolution would then be favored
in pastes with a higher amount of binder, considering that we were
working under the saturation concentration. As is shown in
Figure 3b. the peak current increases when the binder amount
increases. The smaller magnitude of the peak currents observed for
the fs method, show that the dissolution process has gone farther for
this method. The same results were observed when using silicone
Determination of the amount of ferrocene oxidized during the
anodic potential sweep was carried out by evaluation of the
electrical charge (Q) associated with the process. Q was calculated
from the area of the oxidation peak and related to the number of
where F is the Faraday constant, n is the number of electrons
involved in the oxidation process and N is the number of moles of
dependence of Q and the transformed moles with the sweep rate,
for the two manufacturing methods studied with different amounts
of Nujol. The charge decreases with the sweep rate, and two regions
could be observed: at slow rates, where the quantity of ferrocene
transformed depended on the binder content, and another region
at high rates, where ferrocene transformation was practically
independent of binder content.
The slow rate region indicates the participation of the dissolved
ferrocene species: whereas the fd preparation exhibits smaller
transformation when the binder content is increased (Fig. 4a) ,for
the fs preparation the opposite effect was observed (Fig. 4b).
Apparently, as the rate decreases, a signiﬁcant supply of material
was achieved by diffusion of the dissolved species, and more
ferrocene was involved in the transformation. When the rate
increased, there was not enough time for the diffusion to supply
more species. The ferrocene transformed in this case was mostly
present at the electrode–electrolyte interface. The higher amount of
ferrocene transformed in the fd preparation at slow rates supports
Analysis of the amount of moles transformed in the oxidation
process was very enlightening. The results demonstrated unambigu-
ously that the number of moles involved in the process (to the order

in graphite) in a CPE with Nujol as the binder. Support electrolyte: 2 M

different ways: a) the ferrocene was previously dissolved in the Nujol and the
graphite was added afterwards, b) the ferrocene and the graphite are mixed
and then the binder was added. Nujol content: –) 0.35 mL, K) 0.45 mL, ¬)
of 10¹10) was much smaller than the amount found in the electrode
(5 × 10¹5 mol), indicating that only a superﬁcial layer was involved
in the process, as previously reported [3, 28]. Nevertheless, we
calculated the amount of ferrocene present on the electrode surface,
estimating that the quantity transformed was higher both for the
hypothetical case, where the electrode surface is totally covered
with ferrocene molecules (3 × 10¹11 moles), or for the case where
the surface is occupied by the three electrode components: graphite,
ferrocene, and binder (4.47 × 10¹14 moles). To calculate these
values we considered the area occupied by a ferrocene molecule,
1.68 × 10¹15 cm2, (estimated with aid of a software for visualization
and computation of electronic structures by Company Wave
0.031 cm2. These ﬁndings indicate that not only the ferrocene
located at the electrode surface could undergo transformation, but
also those molecules in more internal layers of the electrode must
have been involved. This effect was more evident in studies at low
rates due to the contribution of diffusion of the Fc(d) species in the
3.3. Study of the Role of Solid Ferrocene Species in the
Aiming to outline the contribution of the solid species to the
process, experiments were carried out in which the amount of
electroactive species was increased from 0.5 % to 3 %, keeping the
amount of binder constant at 0.55 mL. The fs preparation alone was
used for this purpose, as it was to be expected that the solid
ferrocene contribution on the paste could be greater. Figure 5 shows
three typical voltammograms for CPE’s with silicon oil and various
ferrocene contents registered at 100 mV/s and 10 consecutive
potential cycles. A single oxidation and reduction peak is observed,
corresponding to the ferrocene/ferrocinium redox process. Differ-
ences on the shape of the curves can be observed: during the cycling
process, the current of the peak varies from one cycle to another,
and the anodic peak in subsequent cycles get narrower as the
amount of ferrocene in the paste increases.
The width of the peak was evaluated in reference to the potential
difference (DE) between the anodic peak current potential (Epa) and
its half peak potential (Epa=2). Table 1 shows DE for different
ferrocene content in the electrodes at sweep rates of 40 and
100 mVs¹1, for each of the two binders used. For pastes with low
ferrocene content, (up to 1 % in Nujol and up to 0.5 % in silicone
oil) peaks have the same width in every cycle; where as when the
amount of ferrocene was increased, the ﬁrst cycle was always wider
and the difference in width for subsequent cycles was more
apparent when the ferrocene content was higher. Also, the shape of
the peaks observed when silicon oil was used as a binder were
narrower than those observed for Nujol.
These results suggest that the ﬁrst cycle involves mainly the
oxidation of a soluble and the subsequent cycles that of an insoluble
species, since narrow peaks are characteristic of solid species [29].
A contradiction arises from this: the linear dependence of ipa on v1/2
(average r ¼ 0.97) for the cycles subsequent to the ﬁrst cycle
indicates a diffusional process; however, this is not enough
evidence to eliminate the possibility of a solid species involved in
the process: this could mean that contributions of both species were
not entirely separated for one another.
Assuming that the oxidation of Fc(d) to ferrocinium (Fcþ) is
possible, calculations to compute the standard potential of this
process were carried out. For these calculations, the estimated
solubilities were used and it was assumed a dissolution equilibria
for a nonpolar solvent. As a result of this study, it was observed that
both oxidation processes [ Fc(d) and Fc(s) to Fcþ] differ only in 75
and 50 mV in Nujol and silicone oil, respectively. This small
difference makes it impossible to observe energetically separate
processes in terms of energy [30] and only one peak is observed in
Carbon Paste Electrodes with Nonconducting Binders

the anodic region during the voltammetric study. A different study
was required to separate the two processes.
A graphite electrode was manufactured with epoxy resin;
material that would not allow ferrocene to dissolve. This could
provide information in terms of the role of solid ferrocene in the
oxidation process. In order to obtain a similar response as the one
for CPE’s in terms of current, it was required to increase the
ferrocene content up to 45 % due to resistance effects. Figure 6
shows a typical voltammogram with anodic and cathodic response
for the oxidation process for ferrocene/ferrocinium. A more
complete study on the curves obtained will be the object of a
future publication. Considering only solid ferrocene was present,
this was the species that was accounted for the electrochemical
response and for the shape of the peaks in subsequent cycles
(narrow anodic peak DE ¼ 30 mV). This oxidation process has been
thought of as being the same as the one responsible for the narrow

peaks in Figure 5. In this ﬁgure, the anodic peak includes a mixture
of peaks from the solid and dissolved ferrocene species. The
contribution of the solid species was obvious for the case of silicon
oil which is precisely the binder in which ferrocene is less soluble. It
seems to be that during the ﬁrst potential cycle, the role of the
dissolved species was predominant because there was enough time
for it to be formed, whereas in subsequent cycles the solid species
has a more important contribution maybe because from one cycle to
the next there might not be enough time to replenish the initial
conditions in the interphase. A second cathodic peak of smaller
magnitude IIc can be observed at high ferrocene content but only
when numerous potential cycles were carried out. The variation of
the sweep rate has also had an effect on the shape of the curves
when different binders were used (Fig. 7). Mostly, a single peak in
oxidation and reduction was observed, but the small IIc peak
observed before in pastes with silicon oil binder is observed here
from the ﬁrst cycle when the sweep is done at slow rates. This
process is thought to be a result of the reduction of ferricinium to
Carbon Paste Electrodes with Nonconducting Binders
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 5
Fig. 7. Typical cyclic voltammograms for solid ferrocene (2 % w./w. in graphite) in an CPE, using Nujol and silicon oil as binders. The ﬁgure shows 10 cycles
and three different sweep rates. Binder content: 0.55 mL. Support electrolyte: 2 M H2SO4. The sweep rates and the number of each cycle are indicated in the
ferrocene [27], and even though it points towards a signiﬁcative
difference in the use of the two different binders, it wasn’t the object
Variations in the peak current during the potential cycling that
indicate differences in the ferrocene concentration in the interphase,
were also observed. This tendency can be seen in Figure 7.
Voltammograms for Nujol show that at high rates, the peak
current increases slightly with the cycling, whereas at slow rates, it
decreases. Intermediate rates present a combination of increases
and then decreases in current. Pastes with silicon oil show this same
behavior at slow rates. The increase in the current with the number
of cycle was more obvious when the ferrocene content was
increased, and much more apparent in pastes with silicone oil. We
have thought that this could indicate the relative contributions of
solid or dissolved species of ferrrocene. We assume that the
increase in the current in subsequent cycles to the ﬁst one indicates
a progressive accumulation of solid ferrocene in the interphase, that
results from the reduction of ferrocinium formed in the initial
oxidation process, as it is favored in the binder in which the
ferocene presents a lower solubility. The decrease in the current
affect in the ﬁrst cycle could be a result of a time-lapse between
cycles that would allow the dissolution process of ferrocene in the
binder. This accumulation of the oxidation or reduction products in
the interphase after numerous cycles has already been mentioned by
other authors in this type of electrodes [31].
The voltammetric behavior of ferrocene contained in a CPE with
nonconductive binder depends considerably on the composition of
the paste and the method used to manufacture the electrode. Using
cyclic voltammetric techniques, these parameters were tested and a
single peak containing contributions of a solid and a dissolved
ferrocene species was observed. It has been shown that indis-
criminate use of binders in CPE’s should be taken into considera-
tion, due to component interactions in the paste. This was observed
in two commonly used binders in which electroactive species can be
dissolved at a different extent. The amount of binder and
electroactive species affect the process, and it could be a
disadvantage to ignore the analytical determination of soluble
compounds in the binder. From this study, it was possible to
establish that the process is limited by diffusion, resulting from the
ferrocene dissolution in the binder, and that the electrochemical
transformation takes place in a diffusion layer inside the electrode.
The fact that two ferrocene species are involved in the oxidation
process can explain the existing controversy in reported voltam-
metric studies for this species in CPE’s, because of differences in
paste composition and electrode manufacturing processes.
